narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've poured your heart into this journey, and we appreciate the strength and determination it takes to pursue your dreams in a field that needs more voices like yours. Your profile includes an undergraduate GPA of 3.10, an LSAT score of 28.50, Year 1 school decile ranking of 4th, Year 3 decile of 2nd, upper-middle family income, and plans to attend full-time. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on patterns from thousands of past students, estimated a 49.9% chance that you would fail the bar—high enough to tip the decision toward rejecting your application to protect the school's outcomes. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 decile ranking of 2nd, which is notably lower than the typical student's 5.53 average and signaled potential challenges in sustaining strong performance. On a positive note, your undergraduate GPA of 3.10, close to the average of 3.21, strongly pushed the model toward expecting bar passage success. Your LSAT score of 28.50, though below the usual 36.70, also worked in your favor by suggesting enough foundational ability to pass. Smaller influences like your Year 1 decile of 4 (under the 5.71 average), full-time status (common among 92.7% of students), and upper-middle income helped slightly toward success but weren't enough to outweigh the concerns. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your later-year grades stand out as the key area lagging behind, while your GPA holds you closer to the pack. This isn't a judgment on your potential—it's the model's data-driven read on bar risks. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your recent academic record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and seeking mentorship from Black women lawyers who've navigated similar paths to bar success. You're resilient, and with targeted steps, you can reshape this story and reapply stronger. Keep pushing—you belong in law.
